walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Central Action Board
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Caverna set up. First, place the central action board in the middle of the table. This is the big board that holds all the action spaces where you will place your dwarves during the game. Make sure everyone can reach it comfortably.

walkthrough.title: Add Action Strips for Player Count
walkthrough.text: Now check how many players you have. Based on your player count, you will add additional action strips to the sides of the central board. These strips provide extra action spaces so that there are enough options for everyone. With more players, you add more strips. The rulebook has a chart showing exactly which strips to use.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Round Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the round cards. These are divided into stages. Shuffle the round cards within each stage separately, then stack the stages in order with stage one on top. Place the deck face down near the action board. Each round, you will flip the top card to reveal a new action space.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out All Furnishing Tiles
walkthrough.text: This is a big one. Find all forty-eight furnishing tiles and lay them face up on the supply board or near the action board where everyone can see them. Unlike Agricola, there are no hidden cards in Caverna. Every furnishing tile is available from round one, so take a minute to scan what is out there. These tiles will go into your cave spaces during the game.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Landscape Tiles
walkthrough.text: Sort all the landscape tiles into separate piles by type. You will need meadow tiles, field tiles, cavern tiles, tunnel tiles, deep tunnel tiles, ore mine tiles, and ruby mine tiles. Place these in easy reach of all players. You will be placing these on your home board throughout the game to develop your farm and cave.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Resource Supply
walkthrough.text: Now organize all the resource tokens into a shared supply. You will need piles of wood, stone, ore, food, grain, and vegetable tokens. Also set out the ruby tokens separately. There are a lot of resources in Caverna, so take a moment to make sure everything is sorted and accessible.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Animal Supply
walkthrough.text: Sort all the animal tokens into five piles by type. Caverna has five kinds of animals: sheep, donkeys, wild boar, cattle, and dogs. Place them all in a shared supply near the resources. Animals will breed during harvest phases, so you will need easy access to these throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Fences and Stables
walkthrough.text: Set out the fence pieces and stable tokens where everyone can reach them. Fences are used to build pastures on your outdoor terrain to hold animals. Stables can be placed inside pastures to double their animal capacity, or in cave spaces to hold one animal without fences.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Home Boards
walkthrough.text: Give each player one double-sided home board. This is your personal territory with two halves. The left side is your farm, with forests and meadows for growing crops and raising animals. The right side is your cave, with rock spaces for digging tunnels and building furnished rooms. You will be developing both sides throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Dwellings
walkthrough.text: Each player places their starting dwelling tile on the designated cave space on the right side of their home board. This is where your first two dwarves live. You will need to build additional dwellings before you can grow your family. Look at your cave side and find the marked starting position for this tile.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Dwarves
walkthrough.text: Each player takes two dwarf meeples in their chosen color and places them on their starting dwelling. These are your workers. You will place them on the central action board during each round to take actions. You start with two, but you can grow your family up to five or even six dwarves over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Food
walkthrough.text: Now give each player their starting food. The first player gets a small amount of food, and each subsequent player in turn order gets a bit more to compensate for going later. Check the rulebook for the exact amounts based on your player count. Food is critical because you must feed your dwarves at every harvest.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Weapon Tokens
walkthrough.text: Place the weapon tokens near the supply. Dwarves start unarmed, but during the game they can acquire weapons through specific action spaces. Weapon strength ranges from one to fourteen. Armed dwarves can go on expeditions for powerful, flexible rewards that get better as their weapon strength increases.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Begging Tokens
walkthrough.text: Place the begging tokens near the supply where everyone can see them. If you cannot fully feed your dwarves during a harvest, you must take one begging token for each food you are short. Each begging token costs you three victory points at the end of the game. You want to avoid these at all costs.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick a random player to go first and give them the first player marker. Play proceeds clockwise from the first player during each round. The starting player can change during the game if someone takes the Starting Player action space. Ready? Let's learn how to play.

walkthrough.title: The Twelve Rounds
walkthrough.text: Caverna is played over twelve rounds, grouped into stages. Each round follows the same sequence: reveal a new action space, replenish resources on accumulating spaces, then everyone takes turns placing dwarves. The game starts slow with just two dwarves each, but by the end you could have five or six workers taking actions every round.

walkthrough.title: Round Sequence Overview
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, flip the top round card and place the new action space it describes on the board. Then add resources to any action spaces that show accumulation icons. These spaces get better each round they go unused. After that, the work phase begins where players take turns placing dwarves.

walkthrough.title: The Work Phase
walkthrough.text: Starting with the first player and going clockwise, each player places one dwarf on an available action space and immediately resolves that action. Then the next player goes. Keep going around the table until every player has placed all of their dwarves. Each action space can only hold one dwarf per round, so choosing quickly matters when a space you need is about to be taken.

walkthrough.title: Your Home Board: Two Halves
walkthrough.text: The left side is your farm where you grow crops and raise animals. It starts with forest and meadow spaces. The right side is your cave where you dig tunnels, build mines, and furnish rooms. Both halves have empty spaces, and every empty space at game end costs you one victory point. You need to fill both sides.

walkthrough.title: Farming Actions: Plow and Sow
walkthrough.text: Let's start with the farm side. To grow crops, first use an action to plow a field, which converts a meadow space into a field. Then use a sow action to plant grain or vegetables on your empty fields. When you sow grain, you place three grain tokens on the field. Vegetables place two tokens. During each harvest, you remove one token from each sown field. That harvested crop goes into your supply.

walkthrough.title: Farming Actions: Build Pastures
walkthrough.text: To keep animals on your farm, you need pastures. Use an action to build fences around meadow or forest spaces on your outdoor terrain. Each fenced space in a pasture holds two animals of the same type. You can add a stable to a pasture space to double its capacity to four animals. A single unfenced meadow can also hold animals if you have dogs to guard sheep there.

walkthrough.title: Animals and Dogs
walkthrough.text: Caverna has five animal types: sheep, donkeys, wild boar, cattle, and dogs. Dogs are special because they act as shepherds. One dog in a meadow space allows you to keep up to two sheep there without fences. More dogs means more sheep. Donkeys are also special because they can live in ore mines, one per mine, without needing pasture space. Animals breed during harvest if you have at least two of the same type.

walkthrough.title: Cave Actions: Dig Tunnels
walkthrough.text: Now let's look at the cave side. Your cave starts as solid rock. Use tunnel actions to convert rock spaces into tunnel tiles. Tunnels are the foundation for everything underground. Once you have tunnels, you can upgrade them into either mines or furnished caverns. Deep tunnels can be dug further into the mountain for access to more valuable upgrades.

walkthrough.title: Cave Actions: Build Mines
walkthrough.text: Tunnels can be converted into ore mines or ruby mines. Ore mines are worth three victory points each at game end, and donkeys can live inside them. Ruby mines are worth four victory points each and produce one ruby per harvest. Rubies are incredibly flexible and can be converted into almost any resource you need.

walkthrough.title: Cave Actions: Furnish Caverns
walkthrough.text: You can also convert tunnels into caverns and place furnishing tiles on them. There are forty-eight furnishing tiles available from the start of the game, and they provide a wide range of benefits. Some are dwellings that give you living space for new dwarves. Others are parlors that score bonus points at game end. Storage rooms give you resource benefits, and chambers provide special abilities.

walkthrough.title: Furnishing Tile Types
walkthrough.text: Dwellings are essential because you need living space before you can grow your family. Parlors give you end-game scoring bonuses based on various conditions. Storage rooms help you manage resources more efficiently. Chambers provide ongoing special abilities during the game. Each tile can only be claimed by one player, so if you see a tile that fits your strategy, grab it before someone else does.

walkthrough.title: Arming Dwarves and Expeditions
walkthrough.text: One of the most exciting features in Caverna is expeditions. First, you need to arm a dwarf using an action space that provides a weapon. Each weapon has a strength value starting at one. Once armed, that dwarf can go on expeditions instead of normal actions. During an expedition, you choose rewards from a list based on weapon strength. Higher strength means more reward options and better choices.

walkthrough.title: Expedition Rewards
walkthrough.text: These are incredibly flexible. Depending on your weapon strength, you can gain resources, acquire animals, build fences, sow fields, place furnishing tiles, and much more. After each expedition, your dwarf's weapon strength increases by one, making future expeditions even more rewarding. Armed dwarves become your most versatile workers over time.

walkthrough.title: Family Growth
walkthrough.text: Growing your family is critical because more dwarves means more actions each round. But there is a prerequisite: you must have an unoccupied dwelling space before you can add a new dwarf. Dwellings come from furnishing tiles placed in your cave. Plan ahead and build a dwelling before you try to grow your family, or you will waste the action.

walkthrough.title: The Harvest Phase
walkthrough.text: Harvest phases happen at the end of certain rounds, marked on the round cards. Each harvest has three steps that always happen in order. First is the field phase, then the feeding phase, then the breeding phase. Harvests become more frequent as the game progresses, so you need a reliable food engine in place by the midgame.

walkthrough.title: Harvest: Field Phase
walkthrough.text: During the field phase, you remove one grain or vegetable token from each of your sown fields and add it to your supply. This is your crop harvest. Fields that still have tokens on them will produce again next harvest. Once a field is empty, you will need to sow it again to get more crops.

walkthrough.title: Harvest: Feeding Phase
walkthrough.text: Now comes the pressure. Each dwarf must be fed two food. Newborn dwarves gained this round only need one food. You can convert various resources into food. Grain can be baked into food. Animals can be slaughtered for food. Rubies can be traded for food. If you cannot feed all your dwarves, you take one begging token per missing food, and each begging token costs three victory points.

walkthrough.title: Harvest: Breeding Phase
walkthrough.text: After feeding, your animals breed. For each animal type where you have at least two of the same kind, you gain one additional animal of that type from the supply. This is free and automatic. Breeding is powerful because it compounds over time. Two sheep become three, then four, then five. But you must have space for the newborns or you lose them.

walkthrough.title: Rubies: The Wildcard
walkthrough.text: Rubies are the most flexible resource in Caverna. You can convert a ruby into almost anything: food, resources, animals, or even use them to take special actions. Ruby mines produce one ruby per harvest, giving you a recurring source of flexibility. When in doubt about what to take, rubies are rarely a bad choice.

walkthrough.title: End of Game Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the harvest in round twelve. Scoring has many categories. You score points for each animal type based on quantity, with zero of any type costing you negative two points. You score for grain and vegetables, with zero of either costing negative one point. Rubies are worth one point each. Each dwarf is worth one point.

walkthrough.title: Scoring: Board Coverage
walkthrough.text: Every unused space on your home board costs you negative one victory point. This includes both the farm side and the cave side. With a large board to fill, leaving spaces empty adds up quickly. Pastures score two points per fenced area. Ore mines score three points each. Ruby mines score four points each. Furnished caverns score their printed tile values.

walkthrough.title: Scoring: Penalties and Tiebreaker
walkthrough.text: Begging tokens cost three victory points each. Gold coins are worth one point each. Parlor furnishing tiles provide additional end-game scoring bonuses based on their specific conditions. Add everything up. The player with the highest total wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most animals wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Mountain
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with four example players. Each player will take different actions so you can see how everything works. Player 4 will make a few common beginner mistakes so you can learn what to avoid. Let's head into the mountain.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 Setup
walkthrough.text: Everyone starts with two dwarves and a small supply of food. Flip the first round card to reveal a new action space on the board. Then replenish any accumulating spaces with their listed resources. In round one there are not many action spaces yet, so your choices are limited. That is fine. Let's make the most of what we have.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Gather Wood
walkthrough.text: Player 1 places a dwarf on the wood gathering action space. This space has been accumulating wood since the start of the game, so Player 1 picks up all the wood tokens sitting there. Wood is essential for building fences, stables, and some furnishing tiles. A strong early resource grab like this gives you options for your next few turns.

walkthrough.title: Why Gathering Resources Early Matters
walkthrough.text: In the first few rounds, accumulating spaces have been building up resources. Grabbing a large pile of wood, stone, or ore early gives you a head start. These resources fund your building projects for the next several turns. Experienced players often race for the biggest accumulation piles in round one.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Dig a Tunnel
walkthrough.text: Player 2 places a dwarf on the tunnel digging action space. They take a tunnel tile and place it on a rock space on the right side of their home board. This opens up the cave for future development. Tunnels are the foundation for mines and furnished caverns, so starting early on cave development is smart.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn: Plow a Field
walkthrough.text: Player 3 takes the action to plow a field. They convert a meadow space on the left side of their home board into a field. Fields are where you grow grain and vegetables. Player 3 is setting up for future sowing actions to get crops producing food for harvest.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Turn: Attempt Family Growth
walkthrough.text: Player 4 gets excited and wants to grow their family right away. More dwarves means more actions, right? But wait. Player 4 only has the starting dwelling, which is already full with two dwarves. There is no unoccupied dwelling space available. Player 4 cannot take the family growth action. This turn is wasted because they did not plan ahead.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Dwellings Before Growing
walkthrough.text: The takeaway here is critical. You must have an unoccupied dwelling space before you can add a new dwarf to your family. Dwellings come from furnishing tiles placed in your cave. Before you try to grow your family, make sure you have dug a tunnel, converted it to a cavern, and placed a dwelling tile there. Plan at least two turns ahead for family growth.

walkthrough.title: Player 4 Recovers: Gather Stone
walkthrough.text: Stone is useful for building dwellings and other furnishing tiles. This is a fine backup plan. Player 4 now has stone saved up for when they are ready to build a dwelling and then grow their family.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Second Turn: Build Fences
walkthrough.text: Player 1 places their second dwarf on a building action. Using the wood they gathered earlier, they build fences on their farm to create a pasture. This enclosed area can now hold two animals. If Player 1 adds a stable later, it will hold four. Getting pastures up early prepares you for animal acquisition and breeding.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Second Turn: Furnish a Cavern
walkthrough.text: Player 2 already dug a tunnel on their first turn. Now they convert that tunnel into a cavern and place a dwelling furnishing tile on it. This costs some resources, but Player 2 now has living space for a third dwarf. Next round, Player 2 will be ready to grow their family. That is great planning.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Second Turn: Sow Crops
walkthrough.text: Player 3 plowed a field on their first turn. Now they sow grain on that field, placing three grain tokens on it. Each harvest, one grain will come off that field. Grain can be baked into food for feeding or left to score points at game end. Player 3 is building a reliable food engine through farming.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Ignoring the Cave
walkthrough.text: Player 4 places their second dwarf on another farming action, getting some sheep for their outdoor terrain. But look at Player 4's cave side. It is completely untouched. No tunnels, no caverns, no mines, nothing. Every single rock space on that side will cost negative one victory point at the end of the game. That is a lot of lost points that Player 4 is not thinking about.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Develop Both Sides of Your Board
walkthrough.text: Caverna punishes you for ignoring half of your board. Every empty space on both the farm side and the cave side costs negative one victory point. If you focus only on farming and leave your entire cave undeveloped, you could lose eight or more points from empty cave spaces alone. Make sure you are digging tunnels, building mines, and placing furnishing tiles throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: End of Round 1
walkthrough.text: All dwarves have been placed and their actions resolved. Now return all dwarves to their home dwellings. Check whether this round triggers a harvest. Round one typically does not have a harvest, so we skip straight to round two. But keep in mind that harvests will come soon, and you need food ready.

walkthrough.title: Round 2: New Action Space
walkthrough.text: Replenish all accumulating spaces. Now the board has more options than round one. With more action spaces available, players have more strategic choices. Let's continue the tutorial with a few more key turns.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Family Growth
walkthrough.text: Since they have an unoccupied dwelling space, they successfully add a third dwarf. Next round, Player 2 will have three dwarves to place instead of two. That is a fifty percent increase in actions. Growing your family early is one of the most powerful moves in the game.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Arm a Dwarf
walkthrough.text: Player 1 takes a weapon action, arming one of their dwarves with a weapon of strength one. This dwarf can now go on expeditions in future rounds. Expeditions provide flexible rewards that scale with weapon strength, and the weapon gets stronger after each expedition. Arming a dwarf early means more expeditions over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: No Food Plan
walkthrough.text: A few rounds later, the first harvest arrives. Player 4 has three dwarves now, needing six food total. But Player 4 has been spending resources on pastures and animals without planning for the feeding phase. They only have two food available. Player 4 is short four food and must take four begging tokens. That is negative twelve victory points. Devastating.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Plan for Harvest
walkthrough.text: Feeding is not optional. Each dwarf needs two food at every harvest, and harvests become more frequent as the game goes on. You need a reliable food engine before you start expanding. Grain fields, animal slaughter, cooking furnishing tiles, and expeditions are all ways to generate food. Count your dwarves, multiply by two, and make sure you can hit that number before every harvest.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues through twelve rounds with harvests becoming more frequent. Each round reveals a new action space, giving you more options as the game progresses. Keep developing both sides of your board, grow your family when you have dwelling space, and always plan ahead for feeding. When you have placed all dwarves and resolved the final harvest after round twelve, the game is over.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring Reminder
walkthrough.text: At game end, tally up all scoring categories. Animals, grain, vegetables, rubies, dwarves, gold, furnished caverns, pastures, and mines all give you positive points. Empty spaces, begging tokens, and missing animal types give you negative points. The most common surprise for new players is how much those empty cave spaces and begging tokens hurt. Check your board carefully.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have walked through the core actions of Caverna and watched Player 4 learn three important lessons. Build dwellings before trying to grow your family. Develop both your farm and your cave to avoid empty space penalties. And always plan your food supply before harvest arrives. You are ready to dig into a full game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Dig In
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to furnishing tiles, resources, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q and A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Harvests come soon — start planning food

summary.title: Round 2: New Action Space
bullets: Flip next round card → new action space added
Replenish accumulating spaces
More options available each round

summary.title: Player 2: Family Growth
bullets: Has dwelling space → successfully adds 3rd dwarf
50% more actions next round (3 instead of 2)
Growing family early is one of the most powerful moves

summary.title: Player 1: Arm a Dwarf
bullets: Gives dwarf weapon strength 1
Enables expeditions for flexible rewards
Weapon gets stronger (+1) after each expedition

summary.title: Player 4's Mistake: No Food Plan
bullets: 3 dwarves need 6 food at harvest, Player 4 has only 2
4 begging tokens = -12 VP — devastating
Spent resources on expansion without planning for feeding

summary.title: Lesson: Always Plan for Harvest
bullets: Each dwarf = 2 food per harvest, newborns = 1
Food engines: grain fields, animal slaughter, cooking tiles, expeditions
Count dwarves × 2 and verify food supply before every harvest

summary.title: Continue Playing
bullets: 12 rounds total, harvests become more frequent
New action space each round → more options over time
Keep developing both sides, grow family, plan food

summary.title: Final Scoring Reminder
bullets: Positive: animals, crops, rubies, dwarves, gold, tiles, pastures, mines
Negative: empty spaces (-1), begging (-3), missing animal types (-2)
Empty cave spaces and begging are biggest new-player surprises

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Practiced core actions: gather, dig, build, farm, grow family
Player 4's lessons: build dwellings first, develop both sides, plan food
Ready for a full game!

summary.title: Before You Dig In
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for tiles and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, let's cover the biggest mistakes new players make in Caverna. First, ignoring the cave side of your board and getting crushed by empty space penalties. Second, trying to grow your family without building a dwelling first. Third, forgetting to plan food for harvest and getting begging tokens. Fourth, spreading too thin and trying to do everything at once instead of focusing. Fifth, underestimating how much expeditions can do for you. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the worst beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Feed Your Dwarves First
walkthrough.text: The number one priority in Caverna is making sure you can feed your dwarves at every harvest. Each dwarf costs two food, and begging tokens are negative three points each. That is the harshest penalty in the game. Before you expand your family or build fancy rooms, set up a food engine. Grain fields that produce every harvest, animals you can slaughter, cooking furnishing tiles, or expeditions that bring back food are all reliable options. Count your dwarves, multiply by two, and verify you can hit that number.

walkthrough.title: Grow Your Family Early
walkthrough.text: More dwarves means more actions per round, and more actions compounds over the entire game. Going from two dwarves to three is a fifty percent increase in what you can do each round. But remember, you need dwelling space first. In the first few rounds, prioritize digging a tunnel, furnishing it with a dwelling, and then taking the family growth action. Getting a third dwarf by round three or four sets you up for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Cover Your Board
walkthrough.text: Every empty space on your home board costs negative one victory point at game end. Your board has spaces on both the farm side and the cave side, so leaving areas undeveloped adds up fast. Make a conscious effort to fill both sides throughout the game. On the farm side, plow fields, build pastures, and place stables. On the cave side, dig tunnels, build mines, and place furnishing tiles. Even low-value developments are better than empty spaces.

walkthrough.title: Scan the Furnishing Tiles Early
walkthrough.text: All forty-eight furnishing tiles are visible from round one. Take time before the game starts to scan what is available and think about which tiles fit your plan. Some tiles combo well together. For example, a cooking tile that converts grain to food pairs nicely with a crop-heavy strategy. Dwelling tiles are essential for family growth. Parlor tiles can swing your end-game score significantly. Do not ignore the tile display, and do not wait until late game to realize the tile you wanted is gone.

walkthrough.title: Rubies Are Your Safety Net
walkthrough.text: When you are not sure what to take, rubies are almost always a good choice. They convert into food, resources, animals, and more at favorable rates. A ruby mine produces one ruby per harvest, giving you recurring flexibility. Rubies also score one victory point each at game end if you do not spend them. Think of rubies as insurance. They bail you out when your plans go sideways and they never go to waste.

walkthrough.title: Expeditions Scale Over Time
walkthrough.text: Armed dwarves get stronger after each expedition because their weapon strength increases by one. A dwarf that starts at strength one in round two could be at strength five or six by mid-game. Higher weapon strength unlocks better and more numerous rewards. Arming a dwarf early gives you more total expeditions over the course of the game, and the compounding weapon upgrades make each one more powerful than the last.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Scoring
walkthrough.text: Caverna rewards players who score from many categories. Having zero of any animal type costs negative two points, and having zero grain or vegetables costs negative one point each. A balanced approach that collects at least one or two of every animal, some crops, some rubies, some furnishing tiles, and good board coverage will consistently outscore a player who goes all-in on one strategy. Check the scoring categories and make sure you are not leaving easy points on the table.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't ignore the cave side — empty spaces = -1 VP each
Don't try family growth without a dwelling
Don't skip food planning — begging tokens = -3 VP each
Don't spread too thin early — focus on a core plan
Don't underestimate expeditions

walkthrough.title: Expedition Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: One of the most powerful openings in Caverna is the expedition rush. The idea is to arm a dwarf as early as possible and use repeated expeditions to generate flexible rewards each round. A dwarf armed in round two with strength one can reach strength six or seven by mid-game, unlocking the highest tier of expedition rewards. Expeditions let you take resources, acquire animals, build structures, and even place furnishing tiles all in a single action. The earlier you start, the more you benefit from the compounding weapon upgrades.

walkthrough.title: Building the Expedition Engine
walkthrough.text: To build this engine, prioritize the weapon action space in the first two rounds. Your first expedition will be modest with only strength one or two, but it still provides value. Use early expedition rewards to grab food for feeding and resources for building a dwelling. By round four, you should have a third dwarf and a weapon-carrying dwarf at strength three or four. From here, your expeditions become the primary driver of your economy. Let the expedition rewards cover board development, food, and even furnishing tile placement.

walkthrough.title: Cave Development Strategy
walkthrough.text: The cave-focused approach aims to maximize points from mines, furnished caverns, and parlor scoring bonuses. Ore mines are worth three victory points each, ruby mines are worth four, and furnishing tiles can score four to eight or more points depending on the tile. Ruby mines also produce one ruby per harvest for ongoing flexibility. The key is to dig tunnels aggressively and start converting them to mines and caverns early. By mid-game, you want most of your cave side covered with high-value tiles.

walkthrough.title: Maximizing Cave Points
walkthrough.text: Look for parlor tiles with scoring conditions that match your overall strategy. If you are going heavy on animals, grab the parlor tile that scores for animal variety. If you have lots of dwarves, look for the one that scores per dwarf. Chambers that give you ongoing abilities can fuel other strategies too. The cave approach pairs well with rubies from ruby mines, which fund your feeding and give you flexible resources to cover any gaps. Don't forget that furnished caverns also fill spaces on your board, avoiding the negative one point penalty.

walkthrough.title: Pastoral Farming Strategy
walkthrough.text: The pastoral approach focuses on building extensive pastures and breeding large herds of animals. Animals breed for free at every harvest when you have at least two of the same type. This means two sheep in round four can become five or six sheep by game end without spending any actions to acquire more. Build fences early, add stables to double capacity, and prioritize acquiring breeding pairs of each animal type. Dogs are especially efficient since they guard sheep in unfenced meadows.

walkthrough.title: Breeding Math and Animal Scoring
walkthrough.text: Understanding animal breeding math is key to this strategy. Two animals become three at the next harvest, then four, then five. Starting a breeding pair early means exponential growth over the remaining harvests. Score at least one or two of every animal type to avoid the negative two penalty for missing types. Cattle and donkeys are worth the most per animal in scoring. Dogs pair with sheep for space efficiency. Wild boar can be converted to food in a pinch. The pastoral strategy fills your farm side naturally, but you must still develop your cave to avoid empty space penalties there.

walkthrough.title: Crop Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The crop-heavy approach solves your biggest problem first: feeding. Plow multiple fields and sow them with grain and vegetables. Each harvest removes one crop per field, giving you steady food income. Grain can be baked into food at favorable conversion rates with the right furnishing tile. Vegetables are worth more as food and as end-game points. A player with three or four sown fields rarely worries about feeding and can focus their actions on scoring instead of survival. This frees up your dwarves for more productive actions in the mid and late game.

walkthrough.title: Family Growth Optimization
walkthrough.text: Regardless of your primary strategy, getting to four or five dwarves as fast as possible is almost always correct. Each additional dwarf gives you one more action per round for the rest of the game. If you gain a third dwarf in round three, that dwarf takes nine total actions over the remaining nine rounds. A fourth dwarf in round five still gives you seven extra actions. Build dwellings proactively and take family growth actions as soon as they become available. The action economy of more workers almost always outweighs the cost of feeding them.

walkthrough.title: Ruby Mine Timing
walkthrough.text: Ruby mines are worth four victory points and produce one ruby per harvest. The earlier you build one, the more rubies you collect over the game. A ruby mine built in round four could produce three to four rubies by game end, worth three to four additional points or convertible to critical resources. However, building a ruby mine requires a tunnel and significant investment. The ideal timing is after your third dwarf but before the midgame harvests accelerate. Paired with an expedition dwarf who can help gather the materials, a ruby mine becomes a powerful recurring resource generator.

walkthrough.title: Late Game Point Maximization
walkthrough.text: In the last three rounds, shift your focus from engine building to pure point maximization. Fill remaining empty spaces on your board with anything you can. Make sure you have at least one of every animal type to avoid negative two penalties. Ensure your grain and vegetable totals are positive. Spend any remaining rubies on high-value conversions or keep them for one point each. Place any remaining furnishing tiles you can afford. The difference between a good score and a great score in Caverna often comes down to these final cleanup turns where you plug every gap in your scoring.

entries.definition: A basic furnishing tile placed in a cavern space. Provides living space for one additional dwarf. Required before you can take a family growth action. The simplest and cheapest dwelling option.
A furnishing tile that provides living space for two additional dwarves. More efficient than two simple dwellings but costs more resources. Ideal for rapid family growth.
A dwelling furnishing tile that can be built later in the game when regular dwelling options may already be taken. Provides one living space for family growth.
A higher-cost dwelling that provides living space and additional victory points. Worth building if you can afford the resource cost, since it covers a cave space and supports family growth.

Furnishing tiles that provide end-game scoring bonuses based on specific conditions. Examples include scoring for animal variety, number of dwarves, weapon strength, or specific resource totals. Placed in cavern spaces.
A parlor furnishing tile that scores bonus victory points based on the number of sheep you have at game end. Pairs well with a pastoral strategy focused on sheep breeding.
A parlor furnishing tile that scores bonus victory points based on the number of cattle you have at game end. Incentivizes building pastures and breeding cattle.
A parlor furnishing tile that scores bonus victory points based on the total number of furnished caverns you have. Rewards heavy cave development strategies.
A storage furnishing tile that provides benefits related to stone resources. Storage tiles help you manage resources more efficiently throughout the game.
A storage furnishing tile that provides benefits related to ore resources. Useful in strategies that involve heavy mining or expedition preparation.
A storage furnishing tile that provides benefits for holding multiple resource types. Rewards diversified resource collection.
A storage furnishing tile that provides broad resource management benefits. Helps players who accumulate many different resources throughout the game.
A storage furnishing tile that scores victory points based on the total weapon strength of your dwarves. Pairs well with expedition-heavy strategies where multiple dwarves carry weapons.
A chamber furnishing tile that helps with food production or conversion. Chambers provide ongoing special abilities that trigger during the game.
A chamber furnishing tile that enhances mining-related actions. Provides ongoing benefits when you dig tunnels or build mines.
A chamber furnishing tile that enhances animal breeding. Provides additional animals during the breeding phase of harvest or when acquiring animals through actions.
A furnishing tile that provides a dwelling space with the added bonus of allowing an animal to live in the room. Combines living space with animal housing.
A furnishing tile that provides an extra benefit when you take certain cave-related actions. Enhances your cave development efficiency.
A furnishing tile that lets you convert grain or animals into food at favorable rates. Essential for feeding-heavy strategies. One of the most important tiles for avoiding begging tokens.
A basic resource used for building fences, stables, and some furnishing tiles. Often gathered from accumulating action spaces. Essential in the early game for pasture construction.
A resource used for building dwellings, certain furnishing tiles, and other structures. Harder to acquire than wood but necessary for cave development.
A resource used for forging weapons, building ore mines, and some furnishing tiles. Important for expedition strategies. Can sometimes be converted to food.

The resource used to feed your dwarves during harvest. Each dwarf requires two food per harvest. Can be obtained from grain, animals, rubies, expeditions, and various furnishing tiles. Running out means begging tokens at negative three victory points each.

A crop resource sown on fields. Place three grain tokens when sowing. One grain removed per field per harvest. Grain can be baked into food or kept for end-game scoring. Having zero grain costs negative one victory point.

A crop resource sown on fields. Place two vegetable tokens when sowing. One vegetable removed per field per harvest. Vegetables are worth more as food and as victory points than grain. Having zero vegetables costs negative one victory point.

The most flexible resource in the game. Can be converted to food, any resource, or animals at favorable rates. Ruby mines produce one ruby per harvest. Worth one victory point each if kept at game end. The ultimate safety net.
A resource worth one victory point each at game end. Sometimes gained through action spaces or furnishing tile abilities. Cannot be converted to food or other resources.
An animal type worth victory points based on quantity at game end. Can be converted to one food each with a cooking tile. Breed at harvest with two or more. Dogs can guard sheep in unfenced meadows.

An animal type worth victory points based on quantity at game end. Unique among animals because they can live in ore mines, one per mine, without needing pasture space. Breed at harvest with two or more.
An animal type worth victory points based on quantity at game end. Can be converted to food with cooking tiles. Breed at harvest with two or more. Need pasture space like most animals.
An animal type worth the most victory points per head at game end. Can be converted to food at favorable rates. Breed at harvest with two or more. Harder to acquire but very valuable for scoring.

A special animal type that acts as shepherds. One dog in a meadow space allows up to two sheep to live there without fences. More dogs allow more sheep. Dogs can also live in cave dwelling rooms. They do not breed.
An action that converts a meadow space on your farm into a field. Fields can then be sown with grain or vegetables. Essential for crop-based food engines.
An action that places crop tokens on empty fields. Sowing grain places three tokens; sowing vegetables places two. Crops are harvested one per field per harvest phase.
An action that uses wood to enclose spaces on your outdoor terrain, creating pastures for animals. Each fenced space holds two animals. Multiple connected fenced spaces form one pasture.
An action or sub-action that places a stable token on a space. Stables in pastures double animal capacity from two to four per space. Stables in cave spaces hold one animal without needing fences.
An action that converts a rock space in your cave into a tunnel tile. Tunnels can be upgraded to ore mines, ruby mines, or furnished caverns. Foundation for all cave development.
An action that converts a tunnel into an ore mine. Worth three victory points at game end. One donkey can live in each ore mine. Produces ore with certain furnishing tile abilities.
An action that converts a tunnel into a ruby mine. Worth four victory points at game end. Produces one ruby per harvest phase. One of the highest-value single developments in the game.
An action that converts a tunnel into a cavern and places a furnishing tile on it. Provides abilities, scoring, or living space depending on the tile. Costs resources as listed on the tile.

A special action available only to armed dwarves. Choose rewards from a list based on weapon strength. Higher strength allows more and better rewards. Weapon strength increases by one after each expedition. One of the most flexible actions in the game.

An action that gives a dwarf a weapon with a starting strength value. Once armed, that dwarf can go on expeditions. The weapon stays with the dwarf for the rest of the game and grows stronger with each expedition.

A numeric value from one to fourteen representing how powerful an armed dwarf's weapon is. Determines which expedition rewards are available. Increases by one after each expedition. Higher strength unlocks more and better choices.

A phase triggered at the end of certain rounds, marked on round cards. Consists of three steps: field phase (harvest crops), feeding phase (feed dwarves), and breeding phase (animals reproduce). Becomes more frequent in later rounds.
The first step of harvest. Remove one grain or vegetable token from each sown field and add it to your supply. Fields with remaining tokens produce again at the next harvest.
The second step of harvest. Each dwarf must be fed two food. Newborn dwarves from the current round require only one food. Resources can be converted to food. Shortfalls result in begging tokens.
The third step of harvest. For each animal type where you have at least two, gain one additional animal of that type for free. You must have space for newborn animals or they are lost.
Taken when you cannot feed all dwarves during the feeding phase. One token per missing food. Each begging token costs three victory points at game end. The harshest penalty in the game.

A card flipped at the start of each round that introduces a new action space to the board. Round cards are shuffled within stages, so the exact order varies each game, but stages progress in sequence.

An action space on the central board that gains resources each round it goes unused. The longer a space goes unclaimed, the more resources pile up on it. Taking the action collects all accumulated resources.
An action that adds a new dwarf to your family. Requires an unoccupied dwelling space on your home board. The new dwarf is placed on the dwelling and is immediately available for future rounds.
A fenced area on your outdoor terrain that holds animals. Each fenced space holds two animals of the same type. Stables double capacity to four. Pastures score two victory points each at game end.

Each player's personal double-sided board. The left half is the farm with forests, meadows, fields, and pastures. The right half is the cave with rock, tunnels, mines, and furnished caverns. Empty spaces cost negative one victory point each.

At game end, each empty or undeveloped space on your home board costs negative one victory point. This applies to both the farm side and the cave side. Filling your board is one of the most important scoring considerations.

A tunnel tile that can be dug further into the mountain, extending your cave beyond the initial rock spaces. Deep tunnels can be converted to mines or caverns like regular tunnels but access the deeper spaces on the board.
The player who places dwarves first in each round's work phase. Can change during the game if someone takes the Starting Player action space. Going first gives priority on contested action spaces.
